content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 24, 2019

Michele Bagneris
Pasadena City Attorney
100 N. Garfield Avenue, Room N-210
Pasadena, CA 91109

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-189

Dear Ms. Bagneris:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Pasadena City Councilmember
John J. Kennedy regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note
that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the
facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the value of the trip (and any additional items such as a business class upgrade) from
African Express Travel & Tours qualify as income or a gift under the Act?

CONCLUSION

The value of the trip (and any additional items such as a business class upgrade) would
qualify as reportable income from African Express Travel & Tours and is not a prohibited gift.²

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

A tour company, African Express Travel & Tours, has offered to enter into a contract with
Councilmember Kennedy by which he would serve as the "Tour Host" for a two-week Egyptian
tour. This tour is not being organized or paid for by a governmental or nonprofit entity, nor is it

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulation references are to Title 2.

² Under Regulation 18700.1(c)(2)(D)(ii), Councilmember Kennedy may also have a financial interest in any
person who purchases a tour package, if his commission from that sale amounts to $500 or more. While the facts
presented do not indicate that Councilmember Kennedy's total compensation will equal $500 or more for each tour
package sold, we caution that if the final compensation rendered reaches this threshold or a person reaches this
threshold by purchasing multiple tour packages Councilmember Kennedy may be disqualified from taking part in
decisions implicating the person.

File No. A-19-189
Page No. 2

being organized for purposes of public policy. Other members of the public who are not elected
officials also will be part of the tour.

You state that the benefits provided to Councilmember Kennedy are not in the form of a
"gift" of travel. Rather, in exchange for his assistance in finding a minimum of 20 persons who will
pay for and join the tour, Councilmember Kennedy would receive one free tour package for an
Egyptian tour. If 30 participants pay in full, Councilmember Kennedy would receive a free tour
package and a business class upgrade for his international flight. If 45 participants pay in full,
Councilmember Kennedy also would receive an additional free tour package that he can provide to
a person of his choosing. The individual cost of the tour is approximately $5,300 for adults. To
upgrade to business class for the international segment of travel, a participant would need to pay an
extra $5,000. Councilmember Kennedy will not receive any commission as a result of this tour.

In a follow-up communication, you noted that Mr. Kennedy was asked to consider being a
Tour Host long before he was in elective office, based on his interactions with others in the
community and his interest in African affairs. You further state that many others have become Tour
Hosts on trips to Egypt with this organization over the years, on the same terms and conditions, so it
appears to be a common practice for the organization.

You also note that African Express Travel & Tours is based in San Antonio, Texas, and
does not have an office in the City of Pasadena.

ANALYSIS

The Act is designed to prevent a public official from making a governmental decision if the
official has a financial interest in that decision including, but not limited to any decision with a
foreseeable and material financial effect on a source of income or gifts aggregating to $500 or more
in the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87100 et seq.) To prevent conflicts of interest, the
Act requires specified public officials to file an annual statement of economic interests ("SEI"),
where they disclose interests in investments and real property, as well as income and gifts received
in the preceding year. (Section 87200 et seq.) Income is reportable if it equals $500 or more from a
source during the calendar year. Gifts are reportable if it equals $50 or more from a source during
the calendar year. Additionally, any gift over $500 is prohibited. (Regulation 18940.2.)

Gift

The term "gift" is defined, in relevant part, in Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers
a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received," and places the burden on the official to show that the official provided consideration of
equal or greater value in return for the payment.

Income

The Act defines "income," in pertinent part, in Section 82030(a) as:

"[A] payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage,
advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of

File No. A-19-189
Page No. 3

food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer,
reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension
program paid by any person other than an employer, and including any community
property interest in the income of a spouse. Income also includes an outstanding
loan."

Equal consideration means that each party receives benefits of equal value. (In re Burciaga
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 17.) While we have no exact formula to determine whether an official has
provided consideration of equal or greater value, the value of services rendered may be proven by
evidence as to the customary rate of compensation for such services, irrespective of official status.
Also relevant in the determination might be the length of time spent rendering the services, or
whether the services are of the type not readily available from others. This would depend on factors
such as the extent and nature of the services rendered and at what price such services are typically
valued in the market. (Diep Advice Letter, No. A-18-018; see also Matulich Advice Letter, No. I-
97-304 and Tassi v. Tassi (1958) 160 Cal.App.2d 680, 690-691.)

In this case, you have indicated that many others have become Tour Hosts on trips to Egypt
with this organization over the years, on the same terms and conditions. Under these circumstances,
it appears that Councilmember Kennedy will be providing consideration of equal value in serving
as a "Tour Host" and finding the prerequisite number of paying travelers. Accordingly, the value of
the trip (and any additional items such as a business class upgrade) would qualify as reportable
income from African Express Travel & Tours as opposed to a prohibited gift. We do however
caution that Councilmember Kennedy will be disqualified from taking part in any decision with a
foreseeable and material effect on African Express Travel & Tours if he accepts the travel pursuant
to the Act's conflict of interest provisions. (Section 87100 et seq.)³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[Signature]

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

_______________

³ The conclusion that Councilmember Kennedy is providing equal consideration and the value of the trip is
reportable income, applies only to the extent that the travel purchases are made through arms-length transactions. In the
event trip participants have recent, pending, or expected business before the City, this conclusion does not apply and
Councilmember Kennedy should seek further assistance.
